header.title: Graph
header.description: A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. It is the most general data structure — trees, linked lists, and grids are all special cases of graphs. Mastering graphs unlocks BFS, DFS, shortest path, and connectivity algorithms.

howItWorks.title: How graphs work
items: A graph G = (V, E) has a set of vertices V and a set of edges E. Each edge connects two vertices and may be directed (one-way) or undirected (bidirectional).
The adjacency list is the standard representation: a hash map where each vertex maps to a list of its neighbors. Space: O(V+E) — ideal for sparse graphs.
The adjacency matrix is an alternative: a V×V boolean grid where grid[u][v] = true means edge u→v exists. Space: O(V²) — allows O(1) edge lookup but wastes space for sparse graphs.
Add vertex and add edge are both O(1) — just append to the hash map and neighbor list. Remove vertex is O(V+E) because every other vertex's neighbor list must be checked.
Edge lookup (hasEdge) and remove edge are O(degree) in adjacency list — you must scan the neighbor list. An adjacency matrix gives O(1) edge lookup at the cost of O(V²) space.
BFS and DFS both run in O(V+E) — every vertex is processed once and every edge is examined once. This is optimal: you cannot traverse a graph faster than reading it.

variants.label: Undirected Graph
variants.description: Edges have no direction — A–B means both A can reach B and B can reach A. Adjacency is symmetric: if B is in A's list, A is in B's list.
characteristics: Edge (u,v) stored in both adjList[u] and adjList[v]
Degree of a vertex = number of edges connected to it
Connected graph: every vertex reachable from any other
Used for social networks, road maps, dependency graphs

variants.label: Directed Graph (Digraph)
variants.description: Edges have direction — A→B does not imply B→A. Each vertex has an in-degree (edges coming in) and out-degree (edges going out).
characteristics: Edge (u,v) stored only in adjList[u] — one direction only
In-degree: edges pointing TO a vertex. Out-degree: edges FROM it
DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph): directed + no cycles — enables topological sort
Used for dependency resolution, web page links, task scheduling

variants.label: Weighted Graph
variants.description: Each edge carries a numeric weight (cost, distance, capacity). Weighted graphs are the foundation of shortest path algorithms like Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford.
characteristics: Adjacency list stores (neighbor, weight) pairs instead of just neighbor
Shortest path ≠ fewest hops — must account for edge weights
Negative weights require Bellman-Ford; non-negative use Dijkstra
Minimum spanning tree (Kruskal, Prim) finds lowest-weight connected subgraph

variants.label: DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)
variants.structure:
A → B → D → F
↓   ↓        
C → E
variants.description: A directed graph with no cycles. Every DAG has at least one topological ordering — a linear sequence of vertices where every edge points forward.
characteristics: No cycles — DFS will never revisit a vertex via back-edge
Topological sort produces a valid execution order in O(V+E)
Dynamic programming on DAGs: subproblem dependencies form a DAG
Longest path in a DAG is solvable in O(V+E) — unlike general graphs

footerAlerts.title: Adjacency list vs adjacency matrix
items: Adjacency list — O(V+E) space. Add vertex O(1), add edge O(1), hasEdge O(degree), getNeighbors O(1). Best for sparse graphs (most real-world graphs).
Adjacency matrix — O(V²) space. HasEdge O(1), but add/remove vertex requires resizing the entire matrix. Best when V is small and the graph is dense.
Rule of thumb: if E << V², use adjacency list. If E ≈ V² (dense graph), matrix is acceptable.
For weighted graphs: adjacency list stores (neighbor, weight) pairs; matrix stores weights directly with ∞ for no edge.

footerAlerts.title: When graphs shine
items: Shortest path — BFS for unweighted, Dijkstra for non-negative weights, Bellman-Ford for negative weights.
Connectivity — BFS/DFS to find connected components, detect cycles, check bipartiteness.
Dependency ordering — topological sort on a DAG gives a valid task execution order.
Network flow — model capacity constraints as edge weights (Ford-Fulkerson, Edmonds-Karp).

footerAlerts.title: Common interview pitfalls
items: Forgetting visited set in BFS/DFS — causes infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
Using adjacency matrix when V is large — O(V²) space kills memory for 10⁵ vertices.
Assuming undirected when the problem implies directed — always clarify edge direction.
Off-by-one in edge count — undirected edges are stored twice, so edge count = sum(degrees) / 2.

hints: Always start graph problems by clarifying: directed or undirected? Weighted or unweighted? Cyclic or DAG?
BFS = shortest path in unweighted graph. DFS = connectivity, cycle detection, topological sort.
For disconnected graphs, wrap BFS/DFS in an outer loop over all vertices.
